mnemonics.title: Bresenham Decision Rule
mnemonics.content: When P is Negative: NY (Next pixel is lower in Y), When P is Positive: PY (Next pixel goes uP in Y). Remember: 'Negative-Y, Positive-Y-up' or Pk < 0 → y stays, Pk ≥ 0 → y+1

mnemonics.title: DDA Steps Calculation
mnemonics.content: DDA Steps = max(|dx|, |dy|). Remember: 'Steve Jobs Uses Apple' - Steps = max(|Δx|, |Δy|)

mnemonics.title: Circle Algorithm Initial P
mnemonics.content: P₀ for circle = 1 - r. Remember: 'One Radius' or 'One ring (r) to start'

acronyms.usage: Line drawing algorithm that uses incremental calculation based on slope
Efficient integer-arithmetic line drawing algorithm used in computer graphics
Line drawing algorithm that produces smooth lines by varying pixel intensity
The smallest unit in a raster display, commonly called a pixel

visualTips.visualization: Imagine walking up a staircase that represents a diagonal line - each step represents a pixel. Bresenham's algorithm chooses whether to take a flat step or an inclined step to stay closest to the invisible diagonal line.

Think of scan lines as horizontal laser beams passing through the polygon. Where each beam enters and exits the polygon (intersection points), that's where you turn the 'light' on (fill pixels) between those two points.

Picture a tightrope walker (the line) between two buildings. At each step, the walker must choose between going straight or going up-one. The decision parameter tells which choice keeps the walker closest to the ideal path.

analogies.analogy: DDA is like using a GPS that recalculates your route at every intersection using precise coordinates, while Bresenham is like an experienced driver who knows shortcuts using only whole numbers - faster but requires more clever thinking.
Aliasing is like looking at a diagonal fence through a chain-link fence - you only see the fence at certain angles, making it look like a staircase instead of a smooth diagonal line.

Flood fill is like pouring dye into water - it spreads from a starting point to all connected areas of the same color, while boundary fill is like building walls around an area and then filling inside.
Scan-line fill is like using a horizontal paint roller - each pass (scan line) paints from one edge of the surface to the other, but stops at the boundaries.
